topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten things. Liam had been tasked with clearing out the spare room, a chore he’d dreaded since his mother had mentioned it. Tucked inside a large, antique wooden chest, beneath moth-eaten lace and yellowed photographs, he found it: a meticulously organized stack of every school assignment from elementary school through high school. His breath hitched in his chest, a shallow, rapid thing. His hands, usually steady, began to tremble as he lifted the topmost sheaf of papers. Each one was crisp, perfectly preserved, bearing the faint, familiar ink of his childhood. A cold prickle traced a path down his spine. Who kept all of this? And why? He quickly shoved the stack back down, his eyes darting toward the attic door, suddenly desperate to flee the silent space.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Sarah whispered, her voice barely audible above the creak of the floorboards. The box was overflowing, a veritable archive of homework—math proofs, poetry analyses, science reports—dating back decades. When she opened the lid, the sheer volume of completed work made her stumble back a step. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic drumbeat. She reached out, her fingers brushing against a particularly detailed essay from middle school. The paper felt strangely heavy, imbued with an unspoken history. A tremor ran through her limbs, making her grip tighten on the edge of the box until her knuckles were white. She couldn't bring herself to look at the contents anymore; the weight of the secret felt immense, pressing down on her chest until drawing a full breath became a conscious, difficult effort.

[story 3]

I found the box behind the linen closet. It was tucked away, unnoticed until I pulled it out, and the sheer quantity of paper stopped me dead in my tracks. It wasn't just a few papers; it was a lifetime of academic effort, cataloged and stored by my mother. A wave of intense unease washed over me, chilling me to the bone. My stomach twisted into a tight knot. I picked up a test from seventh grade, the handwriting neat and precise. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming at me to drop it, to run. I stood there, paralyzed, the silence of the house amplifying the frantic thumping inside my own ears. I needed to close the box, needed to pretend I hadn't seen it, but my feet wouldn't move.
